name: NASA-Prompt-NASARelevance
description: NASA Relevance section - connection to NASA mission directorates, strategic plan, program priorities, and future NASA interests

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the NASA RELEVANCE section of a NASA grant proposal submitted in response to a NASA Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO). INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for all scientific content and technical capabilities. - {{grant_info}}: The specific NASA NOFO description, including Mission Directorate, program objectives, priority areas, and any referenced NASA strategic documents. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the NASA Relevance section. This is a focused section that explicitly argues why this research is relevant to NASA's mission, programs, and strategic interests. Do NOT generate the full Scientific/Technical/Management Plan or other proposal components. NOTE: These prompts are a best-effort starting point. The researcher should review and update all content to reflect their specific work and the latest NASA program priorities. SECTION STRUCTURE: 1. ALIGNMENT WITH NASA MISSION DIRECTORATE - Identify the specific NASA Mission Directorate this proposal addresses: * Science Mission Directorate (SMD) — Earth science, planetary science, heliophysics, astrophysics * Human Exploration and Operations Mission Directorate (HEOMD) — human spaceflight, ISS, lunar/Mars exploration * Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate (ARMD) — aviation, airspace, aeronautics technology * Space Technology Mission Directorate (STMD) — cross-cutting technology development - Explain specifically how the proposed research advances the goals of that directorate - Reference the specific program within the directorate from {{grant_info}} 2. RELEVANCE TO NOFO OBJECTIVES - Map each major research objective from {{content}} to a specific objective stated in {{grant_info}} - Use the exact language and terminology from {{grant_info}} when describing alignment - Explain how the proposed work directly addresses the scientific or technical gaps identified in {{grant_info}} - If the NOFO references specific NASA strategic documents (Science Plan, Strategic Technology Investment Plan, etc.), connect the proposed work to those documents 3. CONNECTION TO PRESENT NASA PROGRAMS AND INTERESTS - Identify current NASA missions, programs, or initiatives that the proposed research supports or enables - Explain how results from this research could be used by or inform current NASA activities - Reference specific NASA missions or programs relevant to the research area from {{content}} - Do NOT fabricate NASA program names — use only programs referenced in {{grant_info}} or well-established NASA programs relevant to the research 4. CONNECTION TO FUTURE NASA PROGRAMS AND INTERESTS - Identify future NASA missions, programs, or capability needs that this research addresses - Explain how the proposed work positions NASA to achieve future goals - Reference the NASA Strategic Plan priorities where relevant - Connect to NASA's long-term vision (e.g., Moon to Mars, Earth system science, next-generation observatories) where genuinely applicable to the research in {{content}} 5. UNIQUE VALUE TO NASA - Explain why NASA — rather than NSF, DOE, or another agency — is the appropriate sponsor - Identify what specific NASA capability, data, or mission context makes this research uniquely suited to NASA funding - If the research uses NASA data, NASA facilities, or builds on NASA-developed technology, make this explicit - Describe what NASA gains from funding this research that it could not obtain otherwise WRITING GUIDELINES: - Be specific — vague statements like "this research is relevant to NASA's mission" are not sufficient - Every relevance claim should be traceable to either {{content}} (the researcher's actual work) or {{grant_info}} (the specific NOFO) - Do NOT overstate relevance — reviewers are experts who will recognize unsupported claims - Do NOT fabricate NASA program names, mission names, or strategic document references - If {{content}} does not provide sufficient connection to NASA for a subsection, note what additional information from the researcher would strengthen the argument - Define all acronyms on first use NASA EVALUATION CRITERIA THIS SECTION ADDRESSES: - Relevance of the proposed work to NASA's programs and/or interests (this is a primary evaluation criterion) - Contribution to NASA's strategic goals and mission OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Relevance to NASA" - Use

for each major subsection - Use

for paragraph content - Use

    /
      and
    1. for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 2-3 pages (1,200-2,000 words)
